coredata.dc:description: In the face of global climate change, there has been an increasing focus on sustainable agriculture. The interaction between modern agricultural research and materials science presents opportunities to manipulate plants at the atomic level. With the rapid advancement of nanotechnology in agriculture, researchers have recognized the potential of nanomaterials to regulate plant physiological and biochemical processes, facilitate efficient chemical delivery, and monitor growth status in real-time. However, only a limited range of material properties has been explored among the diverse array of nanomaterials available. In this review, we examine the applications of nanomaterials in plants through the lenses of catalytic properties, morphology, photoluminescence, and remote controllability. We aim to offer new insights into the interactions between nanomaterials and plants while providing strategies for promoting sustainable agriculture.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the face of global climate change, there has been an increasing focus on sustainable agriculture. The interaction between modern agricultural research and materials science presents opportunities to manipulate plants at the atomic level. With the rapid advancement of nanotechnology in agriculture, researchers have recognized the potential of nanomaterials to regulate plant physiological and biochemical processes, facilitate efficient chemical delivery, and monitor growth status in real-time. However, only a limited range of material properties has been explored among the diverse array of nanomaterials available. In this review, we examine the applications of nanomaterials in plants through the lenses of catalytic properties, morphology, photoluminescence, and remote controllability. We aim to offer new insights into the interactions between nanomaterials and plants while providing strategies for promoting sustainable agriculture.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No new data were created or analyzed in this study. Data sharing is not applicable to this article.

ce:sections.ce:para: Advancements in chemistry and materials science have increased the opportunities for researchers seeking to manipulate atoms. In 1959, Richard Feynman introduced the concept of manipulating matter at the atomic level, thereby initiating the historical development of nanotechnology. Today, nanotechnology has made remarkable progress in fields such as medicine (Boisseau and Loubaton, 2011; Vaiserman et al., 2021; Abedini-Nassab et al., 2022) and industry (Singh, 2017). Although engineered nanomaterials applied in agriculture have been extensively reported, further exploration is needed regarding their interactions with plants.

To date, numerous efforts have been made to investigate the interactions between nanomaterials and plants. These studies encompass various fields, including nanomaterial-enhanced biotic and abiotic resistance in plants (Wu et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2018), improved development and light energy utilization (Li et al., 2018; Li, Pan, et al., 2021), enhanced activity of beneficial root microorganisms (Ma et al., 2021), increased chemical delivery efficiency (Zhang et al., 2020),the use of nano-sensors (Wong et al., 2017; Giraldo et al., 2019; Lew et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2020), and enhanced gene-editing efficiency (Mintzer and Simanek, 2009). However, only a limited subset of the diverse range of nanomaterials has been applied in agriculture. In this review, we summarize research on the interactions between nanomaterials and plants while discussing their potentially advantageous features. We hope that this review will contribute to future designs of engineered nanomaterials for sustainable agriculture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Engineered nanomaterials exhibit different biological functions in plants
ce:section.ce:para: Many experiments on plant nanobiotechnology have been conducted, and their mechanisms have also been explored. Nanomaterials exhibit different biological functions in plants because of their different properties ( Fig. 1).

Nanomaterials directly regulate plant growthNanozymes, defined as nanomaterials exhibiting enzyme-like activity, possess the ability to scavenge over-accumulated reactive oxygen species (ROS) (Zhao et al., 2020). Cerium oxide nanoparticles (nanoceria, NC) have been identified as nanozymes in numerous studies (Wu et al., 2017, 2018; Djanaguiraman et al., 2018; Liu, Li et al., 2021). Due to the presence of two valence states (Ce3+ and Ce4+) on their surface, which form an oxygen vacancy (Boghossian et al., 2013), NCs have the ability to effectively scavenge ROS and enhance the salt stress resistance of plants (Wu et al., 2018; Liu, Li et al., 2021). Numerous non-metallic nanomaterials are also believed to mitigate ROS accumulation, such as selenium-doped carbon quantum dots, which can attenuate UV light exposure in plants under salt stress, thereby reducing ROS levels (Li et al., 2024). Furthermore, the structure of certain nanomaterials composed of naturally active molecules exhibits catalytic scavenging properties for ROS (Guo et al., 2024). The initial ROS surge induced by these nanomaterials within plant cells may also enhance the antioxidant capacity of the plants (Chen et al., 2023). Building on this principle, various materials with ROS-scavenging capabilities, including Mn3O4 (Lu et al., 2020), ZnO (Alharby et al., 2016), and silver nanoparticles (Ocsoy et al., 2013), have been discovered and reported. Similar to NCs, Mn3O4 exhibits different valence states that enable efficient ROS scavenging through state transitions. Initially reported for its ROS-scavenging properties in rats, Mn3O4 has since been utilized as an anti-inflammatory agent in vivo (Yao et al., 2018). Furthermore, ZnO has demonstrated nanozyme activity by enhancing antioxidase activity in tomatoes (Alharby et al., 2016), while silver nanoparticles are recognized for their potent antibacterial properties (Ocsoy et al., 2013).

A pertinent question then arises: given that ROS, particularly hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), serve as crucial signaling molecules in plants (Mittler, 2017; Liu, Fu, et al., 2021), can nanozymes modulate this signaling pathway? A study on legumes demonstrated that cobalt ferrite nanoenzyme (Fe@Co) nanoparticles enhanced the nitrogen fixation capacity of root nodules by regulating ROS signals (Ma et al., 2021). Furthermore, nanozymes have been shown to influence plant development; for instance, cotton seeds treated with NC exhibited increased dry weight and accelerated root growth post-germination (An et al., 2020). These effects can be attributed to the ability of nanozymes to regulate physiological and biochemical metabolic processes within plant cells, thereby promoting overall plant growth. In essence, nanozymes function as a regulatory ‘node’ within the biochemical pathways of plants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterials indirectly regulate plant growth as carriers
ce:section.ce:para: Fertilizers and pesticides play a crucial role in ensuring crop production; however, they also contribute to challenges such as increased costs and environmental pollution (Aktar et al., 2009; Campos et al., 2019). Consequently, there is significant interest in developing effective strategies to enhance the utilization efficiency of fertilizers and pesticides while mitigating environmental and biosafety concerns in modern agriculture. In this context, nanocarrier systems offer an efficient, cost-effective, and convenient approach to improving the efficacy of chemical fertilizers and pesticides (Nair et al., 2010; Kah, 2015; Kashyap et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2016).

Fertilizers and pesticides play a crucial role in ensuring crop production; however, they also contribute to challenges such as increased costs and environmental pollution (Aktar et al., 2009; Campos et al., 2019). Consequently, there is significant interest in developing effective strategies to enhance the utilization efficiency of fertilizers and pesticides while mitigating environmental and biosafety concerns in modern agriculture. In this context, nanocarrier systems offer an efficient, cost-effective, and convenient approach to improving the efficacy of chemical fertilizers and pesticides (Nair et al., 2010; Kah, 2015; Kashyap et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2016).

Mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) exhibit excellent stability, a high surface area, tunable pore sizes, easy functionalization, favorable biocompatibility, and an absence of heavy metal contaminants, making them suitable for use in nanocarrier systems for agrochemicals. Researchers have successfully encapsulated salicylic acid within MSNs and applied this formulation to plants to facilitate the controlled release of salicylic acid (Yi et al., 2015). Zinc is a vital trace element necessary for optimal plant growth; however, the efficacy of conventional zinc fertilizers remains suboptimal. Studies have demonstrated that zincated nanoclay polymer composites significantly enhance the delivery efficiency of zinc compared to traditional zinc fertilizers (Liu and Lal, 2015; Kah et al., 2018).

Plants, particularly agricultural species, are subject to environmental changes due to their sessile nature (Choi et al., 2014). Consequently, the judicious application of agricultural chemicals over time is crucial and should be tailored according to environmental factors, such as temperature, salinity, and moisture levels. Nanocarriers present significant opportunities in this context. A study on in vivo agrochemical delivery demonstrated that temperature- and pH-responsive star polymers effectively release chemicals in response to fluctuations in temperature or pH (Zhang et al., 2020). Additionally, polymer-modified mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) facilitate the release of plant hormones triggered by glutathione, thereby regulating plant growth (Sun et al., 2018).

Nanocarrier-mediated transgenic technology offers novel methodologies and insights for gene editing research (Cunningham et al., 2018). The subcellular localization of nanomaterials can vary based on their morphology (Kwak et al., 2019), particle size (Joubert et al., 2013), and surface potential (Hu et al., 2020), enabling targeted delivery of exogenous genes in both plant and medicine research (Zhang et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2019). Martin-Ortigosa et al. (2012) were the first to demonstrate that mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) functionalized with gold could effectively transport bioactive proteins into cells and that plasmids could be delivered using the same carrier system (Martin-Ortigosa et al., 2012).Furthermore, nano-gene carriers have significant applications in plant science studies. The complex formed by small interfering RNA (siRNA) and gold nanoparticles has been investigated with notable efficiency (Lei et al., 2020). This approach is not only efficient but also rapid, providing a new avenue for investigating plant gene functions. Additionally, carbon nanotubes are extensively reported as effective gene carriers utilized in studies of plant gene functions (Burlaka et al., 2015; Herlem et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterials regulate plant growth as converters
ce:section.ce:para: For plants, and particularly for agricultural species, environmental conditions are challenging to modify (Choi et al., 2014). While the canopy microenvironment can be regulated collectively (Tung et al., 2018a, 2018b), certain growth factors, such as light availability, remain difficult to alter (Heskel and Tang, 2018). Nanomaterials offer enhanced selectivity for agricultural production (Djanaguiraman et al., 2018). Carbon quantum dots represent a class of carbon-based nanomaterial (Yan et al., 2018) that are characterized by excellent cellular compatibility (Liang et al., 2013), high optical stability (Wang et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2017), straightforward preparation methods (Song et al., 2018), controllable properties (Li et al., 2015), and cost effectiveness (Nan et al., 2019). Notably, carbon quantum dots can absorb ultraviolet light (Zhao et al., 2013) and emit light in the red–blue spectrum region (Cai et al., 2019).These nanomaterials have the potential to convert low-utilization plant light into high-utilization energy, thereby enhancing light energy efficiency and improving photosynthesis in plants (Li et al., 2018, 2021). Existing research indicates that carbon quantum dots with photonic conversion capabilities can significantly enhance photosynthetic activity in plants. Furthermore, dichlorophenol–indophenol assays conducted with isolated chloroplasts have confirmed that carbon quantum dots improve photosynthesis under standard lighting conditions (Li et al., 2018; Li, Pan et al., 2021).

Plant roots absorb water and mineral nutrients from the soil (White and Brown, 2010), certain small molecular pollutants are taken up and transported to the leaves (Yang et al., 2005). Researchers have developed a method for real-time monitoring of soil pollutants using fluorescence resonance energy transfer, which occurs when two fluorescent materials are in close proximity (<Förster distance) ( Fig. 2). The emission spectrum of one material significantly overlaps with the absorption spectrum of another over a broad range; thus, the fluorescent signal can be quenched via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (Heim and Tsien, 1996; Mitra et al., 1996; Xia and Liu, 2001). In this approach, substance A—capable of specifically binding to pollutants—is utilized to form a complex with single-wall carbon nanotubes. Substance A quenches the fluorescence of monolayer carbon nanotubes through fluorescence resonance energy transfer. When substance A binds specifically to the pollutants, it disrupts the absorption peak’s alignment, preventing further quenching of single-wall carbon nanotube fluorescence. Consequently, we infer pollutant presence by monitoring changes in fluorescence intensity from single-wall carbon nanotubes (Wong et al., 2017). Utilizing this principle allows plant metabolites, such as H2O2, to be monitored using various modified carbon nanotubes (Wu et al., 2020).

Additionally, there is a class of nanomaterials that function as probes. For example, Chen et al. (2018) reported that carbon dots assisted by Ca2+ chelator alizarin red S (ARS) effectively monitored intracellular Ca2+ concentrations. ARS-carbon dots achieve this through fluorescence resonance energy transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Prediction of nanomaterials that can be applied in botany studies
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the aforementioned points, the application of nanomaterials is crucial for their design. We aim to propose potential application methods and discuss which characteristics may be leveraged ( Fig. 3).

These enzymes catalyze numerous biochemical processes within plant cells and serve as regulators of vital biological activities. Nanomaterials can fulfill a similar role in plants, particularly within specific domains. Under certain conditions, such as abiotic stress, membrane-localized enzymes may lose functionality due to membrane damage; however, nanomaterials with analogous catalytic properties remain unaffected. Consequently, we can identify comparable catalytic materials. Inorganic catalysts are efficient and stable, often surpassing carbon-based catalysts (enzymes) in performance (Gao et al., 2007). Nanozyme activity relies on the presence of two-valent ions that create oxygen vacancies with unpaired electrons capable of scavenging ROS (Boghossian et al., 2013; Chen et al., 2021). Various single metal oxides, such as cerium oxide (Wu et al., 2017, 2018; Liu, Li et al., 2021), manganese oxide (Lu et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2021), and iron oxide (Gao et al., 2007), exhibit dual valence states and have been reported to possess active ROS removal capabilities. Additionally, heterostructures formed by binding two metal oxides or through single atomic substitutions based on one crystal structure may result in localized deletions or surface defects. All of these materials can function as nanozymes for regulating ROS signaling.

Nanomaterials also have the potential to produce or scavenge specific signaling molecules while elucidating their roles. ROS are critical signals involved in plant tolerance responses under stress conditions (Mittler, 2017), prompting extensive research into their generation during such events (Zhu, 2016; Wu et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2020). Typically accumulating under stress conditions, ROS, particularly H2O2, can damage proteins or membrane structure (Liu et al., 2021), however, some studies have also indicated interactions with other molecules (Yang et al., 2007; Piacentini et al., 2020; Zhang, Ma et al., 2020). Nanotechnology offers methodologies for investigating how ROS induce plant stress tolerance responses. ROS do not generally accumulate excessively unless catalyzers generate them under light exposure (Xu et al., 2021). This facilitates the exploration of ROS functions in plants while excluding confounding stress factors.

Nitric oxide (NO), a gaseous signaling molecule involved in various physiological processes enhances plant survival strategies against abiotic stresses (Qiao and Fan, 2008; Siddiqui et al., 2011; Mur et al., 2013; Fancy et al., 2017). What is NO’s involvement in enhancing plant resilience against abiotic stress? Nanobiotechnology provides avenues for assessing the potential roles of NO while evaluating its contributions to resistance mechanisms against abiotic stress. Certain catalysts facilitate the conversion of NO into other nitrogen oxides, such as NO2, enabling investigations into plant tolerance without interference from NO itself (Pârvulescu et al., 1998; Choi., 2004; Wang et al., 2021). This approach is similarly applicable when studying hydrogen sulfide (H2S) (Shields et al., 2007; Zhou et al., 2021) and carbon monoxide (CO) (Kapoor et al., 2005).

The shape of nanomaterials varies due to differing synthesis methods (Helmlinger et al., 2016; Khodashenas and Ghorbani, 2019). Distinct morphologies confer unique functions, including chemical loading (Torney et al., 2007; Vashist et al., 2011) and molecular absorption (Thomas, 2008). Mesoporous nanomaterials have been extensively documented as carriers for drug or gene delivery in plant cells (Torney et al., 2007). Similarly, metal framework materials can effectively load chemicals and facilitate directional delivery (Bag et al., 2016). Single-wall carbon nanotubes with tubular structures have also been shown to transfer plasmids into cells (Singh et al., 2005). These nanotubes utilize surface groups within mesoporous materials for plasmid loading (Liu et al., 2009). The surface functional groups of mesoporous materials are typically employed to encapsulate drugs and respond to environmental changes. Several studies have demonstrated that carbon nanotubes can penetrate cell walls and membranes through mechanisms such as landing, piercing, and uptake, potentially occurring via a spontaneous process (Lacerda et al., 2013; Geng et al., 2014), A dendrimer is characterized by a highly branched structure; the functional groups on its branches can be tailored in specific environments to enable effective chemical coating (Ma et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020).

Gas signal transmission represents a form of free molecular diffusion and serves as a crucial mechanism for insect communication (Contreras and Bradley, 2010; Schilman, 2017; Zhang et al., 2020). Blocking the transmission of gas signals may disrupt insect behavior. Activated carbon possesses surface pores that effectively adsorb gas molecules (Zhang et al., 2012). When activated carbon is applied to the surface of insects, it can inhibit the diffusion of these gas molecules. Furthermore, activated carbon materials exhibit excellent biocompatibility and cost-effectiveness (Zhang et al., 2012).To date, the mechanisms by which nanomaterials enter plant cells and the factors influencing delivery efficiency and localization remain poorly understood (Asati et al., 2010; Nair, 2016). Numerous studies have reported the effects of particle size and surface potential on the delivery efficiency of nanomaterials (Wu et al., 2017; Hu et al., 2020). misidentified among organelles or cellular fragments. Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) can determine the elemental composition from observed images (Slater et al., 2016), allowing for some metallic-containing nanomaterials not found in plants to contribute valuable insights into this research area despite high equipment requirements. Nanomaterials exhibiting unique morphologies, such as triangular (Gardner et al., 2011), pentagram-shaped (Xu et al., 2020), and crescent forms (Rochholz et al., 2007), are particularly advantageous for this project due to their more readily recognizable shapes. These investigations have utilized spontaneous fluorescence from the materials, employing laser confocal microscopy to observe the process of nanomaterial entry into plant cells (Hu et al., 2020). However, fluorescent signals may be quenched by soluble sugars or metal ions present within cells (Ankireddy and Kim, 2018; Chen et al., 2018; Lakshmi et al., 2021), potentially interfering with the final results. The subcellular structure of plant cells can be visualized using transmission electron microscopy (Faisal et al., 2016; Mukhoro et al., 2018), however, spherical nanomaterials may easily be misidentified among organelles or cellular fragments. Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) can determine the elemental composition from observed images (Slater et al., 2016), allowing for some metallic-containing nanomaterials not found in plants to contribute valuable insights into this research area despite high equipment requirements. Nanomaterials exhibiting unique morphologies, such as triangular (Gardner et al., 2011), pentagram-shaped (Xu et al., 2020), and crescent forms (Rochholz et al., 2007) are particularly advantageous for this project due to their more readily recognizable shapes.

Photoluminescent materials can be categorized into up- and down-conversion types (Wolfbeis, 2015; Kumar et al., 2016). Down-conversion materials, including carbon quantum dots and carbon nanotubes, exhibit positive Stokes shifts. Conversely, most up-conversion materials with low quantum yields display negative Stokes shifts and often contain heavy metal elements (Wolfbeis, 2015; Kumar et al., 2016), however, the presence of such elements is seldom reported in plant systems (Wolfbeis, 2015; Kumar et al., 2016) This section explores various potential applications of diverse fluorescent materials.

Fluorescent nanomaterials are commonly employed as biosensors. As previously discussed, the fluorescence properties of these materials are influenced by surface functional groups (Chen et al., 2018), allowing the monitoring of concentration changes in specific substrates through complex modification. Single-walled carbon nanotubes and near-infrared emitting quantum dots have been utilized to detect environmental pollutants and metabolites within in plant cells (Wong et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2020). The assessment of infrared light emission effectively mitigates the interference caused by plant auto-fluorescence (Yen Mee et al., 2016). However, high costs remain a significant barrier. In this context, we propose two potential alternatives to conventional fluorescent materials.

Long afterglow materials exhibit fluorescence emission characteristics even after the excitation light is turned off (Pan et al., 2012), with persistence lasting several hours. In contrast, the spontaneous fluorescence of plants typically lasts only a brief period once the excitation light ceases (Singhal and Rabinowitch, 1969; Lei et al., 2017); thus, long afterglow material effectively mitigate background interference. Furthermore, these materials can be excited by sunlight and emit fluorescence in both visible (Kabe and Adachi, 2017) and near-infrared ranges (Lu et al., 2011). Up-conversion materials possess an anti-Stokes shift, enabling them to emit light at shorter wavelengths than that of the excitation source (Auzel, 2004). While numerous studies have reported on nano-biosensors capable of entering cells to monitor metabolites or environmental pollutants, the two fluorescent materials discussed herein may not penetrate cellular membranes due to their larger particle size and presence of heavy metal elements. Nevertheless, they can still be localized within stomata to monitor microclimate changes in situ. Additionally, a type of nanoscale gel that cannot enter cells but can be distributed within intercellular spaces has been documented for observing stomatal structures in certain grass species (Jain et al., 2021).

Fluorescent nanomaterials serve as light energy converters to enhance light energy utilization in plants (Li et al., 2020; Xu, Li, et al., 2020; Xu, Mao, et al., 2020; Li, Xu, et al., 2021). The solar radiation reaching the Earth’s surface is predominantly concentrated in the visible and infrared regions, with a significant distribution within the infrared spectrum, resulting in an overall energy density that is considerably lower than that of visible light (Gueymard, 2004). Plants primarily harness energy from visible light, particularly red and blue wavelengths (Niu et al., 2022). Photosynthesis plays a crucial role in crop yield formation (Graf et al., 2010), and the efficiency of population-level light energy utilization is a key determinant affecting photosynthetic performance (Sager et al. 1982; Li et al., 2000). For many years, researchers have focused on developing new varieties (Liu et al., 2021), optimized plant row spacing configurations, exploring suitable planting densities and sowing times (Khan et al., 2017a; Khan et al., 2017b), and implementing effective chemical control measures to enhance population light utilization rates (Tung et al., 2018a, 2018b, 2020). Fluorescent materials offer additional avenues for improvement; studies indicate that carbon quantum dots can function as effective light converters to boost plant photosynthesis while presenting numerous application opportunities (Li et al., 2018; Xu, Mao, et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020; Li, Pan, et al., 2021; Li, Xu, et al., 2021). Up-conversion materials may also exhibit similar conversion effects (Xu, Li et al., 2020). Certain plants are highly sensitive to photoperiods, such as long-day species, which fail to initiate flower bud differentiation under short-day conditions, posing significant challenges for their cultivation (Adams and Langton, 2005; Song et al., 2013). Although some crops have been successfully domesticated to be less sensitive to light conditions, there remain species resistant to domestication efforts (Cober et al., 1996). Long afterglow materials provide a sustained light source lasting several hours post-excitation, which could facilitate flower bud differentiation in long-day plants (Kabe and Adachi, 2017), Similarly, these materials can store solar energy effectively to extend photosynthetic activity duration.

Photothermal and magnetismSimilar to photoluminescent materials (Wang et al., 2014), certain nanomaterials can be ‘activated’ by exogenous signals (Friend et al., 1999; Yu et al., 2020). Notable examples include photoluminescent (thermal) and magnetic nanomaterials (Mohammed et al., 2017). Longwave radiation exhibits effective penetration through biological tissues (Kang et al., 2019). Gold nanoparticles excited by infrared light can specifically target the rabies virus in murine brains. By irradiating with red light in vitro, these nanoparticles can generate elevated temperatures sufficient to eliminate the rabies virus (Ren et al., 2021). In plants, waterlogging poses a threat as it leads to insufficient soil oxygen levels, resulting in anaerobic respiration (Wang et al., 2009). If nanomaterials within roots are capable of photocatalytically decomposing water and releasing oxygen, this may alleviate hypoxic conditions (Mao et al., 2012), Under chilling stress, damage to the plasma membrane disrupts material transport (Mahajan and Tuteja, 2005). Concurrently, cellular chemical reactions necessitate specific temperature ranges; excessively low temperatures can impede or halt vital metabolic activities (Parkin et al., 1989; Mahajan and Tuteja, 2005). Although various agronomic measures exist to mitigate the impact of low environmental temperatures on plants during agricultural production, advancements in nanobiology offer precise heating solutions for plant cells (Yang et al., 2011; Liu et al., 2016; Waqas et al., 2017), Photo-thermogenic nanomaterials may serve as a heat source for plants to counteract stress induced by low temperatures (Von Maltzahn et al., 2009; Robinson et al., 2011).

A magnetic field can be controlled remotely (Choi et al., 2017; Tang et al., 2018) and cost-effectively, enabling the manipulation of nanomaterial movement (González-Melendi et al., 2008; Gao and Xu, 2009). Carrier platforms utilizing magnetic nanomaterials have been extensively documented in the literature (González-Melendi et al., 2008; Gao and Xu, 2009; Capaldi Arruda et al., 2015). In this context, we propose a potential solution: a self-assembled nanomagnetic stirrer that is formed from raw materials in solution and can be manipulated by an external magnetic field, resulting in observable stirring effects (Ji et al., 2020). Chilling conditions can damage cell membranes, leading to impaired material transport. If a nanomagnetic stirrer is self-assembled within cells and subjected to an external magnetic field for cytoplasmic stirring, the flow rate of the cytoplasm may be enhanced, potentially alleviating chilling stress.

Since Richard Feynman introduced the concept of manipulating matter at the atomic level, it has inspired more precise control and monitoring of physiological activities in plants. Compared with traditional biotechnology, plant nanobiotechnology has more selectivity. However, biosafety is an issue faced by plant nanobiotechnology. At the same time, the high cost may also be a factor limiting the application of plant nanobiology. The inability to stabilize the genetic shape may also contribute to the rising costs. We believe that with the continuous advancement of botany, nanomaterial science, and interdisciplinary research, accurate monitoring of plant growth and directional regulation will become feasible in the future. This development has significant implications for both scientific inquiry and agricultural production. In this review, we present predictions regarding materials that interact with plants based on their physicochemical properties. Although many of these predictions have yet to be reported in the literature, we are optimistic that advancements in technological processes will lead to novel applications for nanomaterials within botanical research.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32060259; 32360547), Xinjiang Production and Construction Corps Southern Xinjiang Key Industries Development Foundation (2022DB015), Xinjiang Production and Construction Corps Natural Science Foundation (2024DB024) and President's Fund of Tarim University (TDZKBS202408; TDZKBS202421).
